text: A famine struck the land, besides the earlier famine in Abraham's time, and Isaac went to Abimelech, king of the Philistines, at Gerar.
The LORD appeared to Isaac and told him not to go down to Egypt, but to stay in the land God would show him.
God told him to sojourn there, promising to be with him and bless him, giving all these lands to him and his offspring, confirming the oath sworn to Abraham.
God said he would multiply his offspring like the stars of heaven and give them all these lands, and all nations would be blessed through his offspring.
This was because Abraham had obeyed God's voice and kept his charge, commands, statutes, and laws.
So Isaac stayed in Gerar.
When the local men asked about his wife, he said Rebekah was his sister, afraid to say she was his wife, since she was beautiful and he feared they might kill him for her.
After a long time there, Abimelech looked out a window and saw Isaac caressing his wife Rebekah.
Abimelech summoned Isaac and said clearly she was his wife, and asked why he had claimed she was his sister.
Abimelech said Isaac could have brought guilt on them, since someone might easily have slept with her.
Abimelech warned everyone that anyone who touched this man or his wife would be put to death.
Isaac sowed crops in that land and reaped a hundredfold that year, and the LORD blessed him.
The man became rich, and his wealth kept growing until he was very wealthy.
He had so many flocks, herds, and servants that the Philistines envied him.
The Philistines stopped up all the wells his father's servants had dug in Abraham's time, filling them with earth.
Abimelech told Isaac to leave them, since he had become too powerful for them.
Isaac left and camped in the valley of Gerar, settling there.
Isaac re-dug the wells dug in his father Abraham's time, which the Philistines had stopped up after Abraham died, naming them as his father had.
Isaac's servants dug in the valley and found a well of fresh, flowing water.
The herdsmen of Gerar quarreled with Isaac's herdsmen, claiming the water was theirs, so he named the well Esek, because they disputed with him.
They dug another well, and quarreled over that one too, so he named it Sitnah.
He moved on and dug yet another well, and this time there was no quarrel, so he named it Rehoboth, saying now the LORD had made room for them and they would flourish.
From there he went up to Beersheba.
The LORD appeared to him that night and told him not to fear, for he was with him and would bless him and multiply his offspring for Abraham's sake.
Isaac built an altar there and called on the name of the LORD, pitched his tent, and his servants dug a well there.
Abimelech came to him from Gerar with his adviser Ahuzzath and his army commander Phicol.
Isaac asked why they had come, since they had hated him and sent him away.
They said they clearly saw the LORD was with him, and wanted a sworn treaty between them.
They asked him to do them no harm, just as they had not touched him but had sent him away peacefully, blessed by the LORD.
Isaac made them a feast, and they ate and drank.
They rose early the next morning and swore an oath to one another, and Isaac sent them away peacefully.
That same day Isaac's servants told him about the well they had dug, saying they had found water.
He named it Shibah, so the city is called Beersheba to this day.
When Esau was forty, he married Judith daughter of Beeri the Hittite and Basemath daughter of Elon the Hittite.
These marriages brought grief to Isaac and Rebekah.
